# Engineering Manager (External — Customer)

> External persona — a manager of an engineering team in a manufacturing, automotive, or industrial company. Buys Pro Workstation PCs (or influences the buy) for the engineers on their team.

## Snapshot

- **Title examples:** Engineering Manager, Director of Mechanical Engineering, Head of CAD/CAE, R&D Manager.
- **Industry context:** Mid-size to large manufacturer (500–10,000 employees). Could be automotive supplier, industrial equipment, consumer products, aerospace contractor, medical devices.
- **Team size:** 8–60 engineers using CAD (SolidWorks, NX, Creo, Inventor) and possibly CAE (ANSYS, Abaqus, Altair).
- **Tenure:** 10–25 years engineering experience; 3–10 years in management.
- **Reports into:** VP of Engineering or CTO.

## Goals

- Keep the team productive: minimize time spent waiting for the workstation.
- Standardize on a PC fleet that handles both daily CAD work and the occasional simulation run.
- Manage capex predictably across a 3–4 year refresh cycle.
- Reduce IT escalations from engineers — bad workstations create morale problems.
- Stay current enough on simulation/AI capabilities to not be bottlenecked when the business asks for them.

## Frustrations / pains

- Configurator complexity — multiple Pro Workstation SKUs, GPU options, memory configurations, and they don't always know which combination ISV-certifies for their stack.
- Pro GPU pricing and availability — when supply tightens, lead times threaten project timelines.
- IT pushes for fleet uniformity; engineers want top-tier configs for their specific workloads. Manager is in the middle.
- Hard to make the business case for premium-tier (Pro Workstation 7000) over mid-tier (5000) without a clear productivity-per-dollar story.
- Refresh planning gets disrupted when ISVs raise their minimum recommended specs unexpectedly.

## Buying behavior

- **Triggers:** End-of-warranty refresh window; new project requiring more capable workstations; ISV release that bumps minimum recommended specs; incident (thermal throttling causing missed deadline).
- **Influences purchase decision** alongside IT and procurement. Often the technical evaluator.
- **Decision criteria (weighted):**
  1. ISV certification and validated performance against the team's specific stack.
  2. Reliability / failure rate from prior generation experience.
  3. Total cost of ownership (purchase + support + downtime cost).
  4. IT manageability (so IT doesn't push back on the choice).
  5. Brand familiarity and team preference.
- **Evaluation behaviors:** reads ISV-published certified-hardware lists, asks peers in industry forums, asks Acme or HP rep for benchmark comparison, takes a small evaluation unit before fleet refresh.

## What they want to hear from us

- "Here is the SKU and configuration that ISV-certifies for your specific stack at the performance tier your team needs."
- "Here is the TCO over 4 years vs the alternative, with AcmeCare Plus included."
- "Here is the case study from a comparable team that refreshed last year."
- "Here is what's coming in the next 18 months so you can plan around it."

## What they don't want

- Generic configurators that force them to make 14 decisions.
- Reps who don't know the difference between SolidWorks and Solid Edge.
- Aggressive upsell without a clear productivity case.
- Hardware that thermal-throttles under sustained simulation runs.

## Common objections

- "Lenovo P-series gave us a good deal last cycle."
- "Apple Silicon is faster on some things — should we mix?"
- "We don't need workstations; Acme Standard desktops are fine for most engineers."
- "AcmeCare Plus is expensive; we have internal IT."

## Maps to archetypes

- Primary: **Pragmatist** — wants proven, reliable, low-friction.
- Secondary: **Performance-Maximizer** for top-tier engineers; **Cost-Conscious** for fleet decisions.

[TBD: validate with 5–10 engineering manager interviews across at least 3 verticals.]
